name: CoQ10 (Ubiquinol)
historicalUse: Coenzyme Q10 was discovered and isolated by Dr. Frederick Crane at the University of Wisconsin in 1957. As an endogenous compound, it lacks classical herbal precedent. However, the cardiac and circulatory tonics of Arabic and Galenic medicine — hawthorn, saffron, rose preparations — addressed the same physiological terrain that CoQ10 now targets at a molecular level, supporting what classical physicians called the vital spirit of the heart.
classicalSources: Contemporary discovery — functional parallels in Ibn Sina's cardiac tonics
modernEvidence: Ubiquinol (reduced CoQ10) is the electron-carrier form in the mitochondrial electron transport chain and a critical lipid-soluble antioxidant in cell membranes. Clinical evidence supports its use in heart failure (NYHA class improvement in Q-SYMBIO trial), statin-associated myopathy (CoQ10 depletion is a documented statin side effect), hypertension, and mitochondrial disease. Ubiquinol form has approximately 3-8x greater bioavailability than ubiquinone.
mechanisms: Mitochondrial electron transport — essential carrier in Complex I-III of the respiratory chain
Lipid-soluble antioxidant — protects LDL and cell membranes from oxidative damage
Endothelial function — supports nitric oxide bioavailability and vascular tone
ATP synthesis — rate-limiting cofactor for cellular energy production in cardiomyocytes
benefits: Supports normal blood pressure already within healthy range
Supports cellular energy production throughout the body
May help maintain CoQ10 levels depleted by statin medications
safetyProfile: Excellent safety profile. Well tolerated at 100–600mg/day. Mild GI discomfort at high doses. Fat-soluble — absorption enhanced with meals containing fat.
contraindications: No absolute contraindications at therapeutic doses
drugInteractions: Warfarin — CoQ10 has structural similarity to vitamin K; some reports of reduced anticoagulation efficacy; monitor INR
Statins — statins deplete endogenous CoQ10; supplementation may be beneficial but does not interfere with statin efficacy
Antihypertensive medications — additive blood pressure-lowering effect; monitor BP
Chemotherapy (doxorubicin) — CoQ10 may protect against cardiotoxicity; discuss with oncologist
physicianNotes: Highly appropriate in patients on statin therapy to address medication-induced CoQ10 depletion. Strong evidence base in systolic heart failure. Ubiquinol form is recommended over ubiquinone for patients over 40 and those with absorption concerns. Monitor INR in warfarin patients.
patientGuidance: Take with your largest meal of the day — CoQ10 is fat-soluble and requires dietary fat for absorption. If you take warfarin, inform your physician. If you take a statin medication, CoQ10 supplementation is particularly relevant for you.
